sentinels.__FORCE00__: Forces effective input value to the hex string '00'.
sentinels.__EMPTY__: Forces effective input value to an empty string.
sentinels.__NULL__: Marker value interpreted by function-specific runtime rules.

functionSpecificRules: musig2_nonce_gen: vals[2] '__NULL__' is translated to null before function call.

typeCoercion.integerParams: Parameters declared as 'integer' in function specs are cast to int when non-empty.
typeCoercion.numberParams: Parameters declared as 'number' in function specs are cast to float when non-empty.

data.content:
# Lesson 15: CoinJoin Classification from First Principles

CoinJoin is not just "equal outputs".
CoinJoin is a cooperative transaction where independent users contribute inputs and receive matching denomination outputs to increase ownership ambiguity.

This lesson answers one practical question:
When a transaction looks mix-like, is it real CoinJoin or a false positive?

## Mental model for this flow

- Structure first: who controls the inputs?
- Pattern second: equal outputs plus change behavior
- Context third: provenance and downstream spending

You will walk three lanes:
- Case A: clean baseline
- Case B: realistic round
- Case C: output-only trap

By the end, you should be able to explain why a case passes or fails, not just quote a metric.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
## How To Read This Canvas

Use the same rhythm as the intro-style lessons: finish one lane left-to-right, then move on.

1. Green lane (Case A): learn the simplest valid CoinJoin shape.
2. Orange lane (Case B): add realistic complexity and change behavior.
3. Purple lane (Case C): study a convincing false positive.
4. Yellow compare lane: combine evidence and make the final analyst call.

Checkpoint question at every stage:
Do multiple independent owners still contribute inputs?
If the answer is no, treat strong output metrics as supporting noise, not proof.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
## Case A: Textbook Baseline Round

Setup:
- 2 participants
- 1 input each (same value)
- 2 equal denomination outputs
- no change branch

Why this case exists:
This is the minimum clean structure where CoinJoin logic is obvious.
Use it to learn the core pattern before adding real-world noise.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
## Case B: Real-World CoinJoin Shape

Setup:
- 5 participants
- equal denomination outputs still present
- change outputs also present

Why this matters:
Real CoinJoin rounds are rarely as clean as Case A.
You must allow normal change behavior while still validating ownership structure, output pattern, and confidence gates.

Case B is the practical positive reference in this lesson.
data.title: Text Info Node
Anonymity Set (count)
data.comment: Output-side anonymity set matches Case B, proving output-only analysis limits.

data.content:
## Case C: Strong Output Signal, Wrong Ownership Model

Setup:
- one owner input
- many equal outputs

Why analysts get fooled:
Output statistics can look excellent: high equal-output ratio and high apparent anonymity set.
But the core assumption is broken: there is no multi-party input contribution.

Interpretation:
Case C reproduces CoinJoin optics, not CoinJoin privacy.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
## Cross-Case Decision Frame

Do not treat this as "highest metric wins".
Use a layered decision order:

1. Ownership test: are there independent input owners?
2. Structure test: do denomination and change behavior look coherent?
3. Risk test: does false-positive risk remain acceptable?
4. Context test: what is effective anonymity after adjustments?

High confidence requires all layers to align.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
### Context Panel: What Basic Heuristics Miss

After baseline checks, apply three context corrections:

- Remix depth
  Higher remix depth can increase resistance to simple chain tracing.

- Script fingerprint
  Script-type consistency can still reveal wallet implementation patterns.

- Effective anonymity
  Apparent anonymity is not final; adjust using provenance and downstream behavior.

This is the difference between "looks mixed" and "defensible analyst conclusion".
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
### Why We Check Both Directions

A >= B and B >= A is an equality proof built from simple comparisons.
Both checks must be true for values to match.

In Case A, this confirms each participant satisfies the same denomination target used by the round.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
### Why Individual Inputs Are Still Visible

The aggregated CSV is great for calculations, but it hides ownership intuition.
So this lane keeps participant inputs visible first, then aggregates them.

Read in order:
1. verify multi-party participation
2. then feed the combined vector into heuristic nodes
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
### Quick Rejection Signal

One large input funding many equal outputs is a common batch-payment footprint.
It can imitate CoinJoin output structure while lacking cooperative ownership.

Core reminder:
Equal outputs are a pattern.
CoinJoin is a coordination model.
data.title: Text Info Node
False Positive Risk
data.comment: Case A is MEDIUM_FP_RISK: tiny set and full uniformity remain ambiguous.

data.content:
### Case A Verdict: Clean Positive, Small Set

What passes:
- ownership structure
- denomination structure
- heuristic gates

What remains limited:
- anonymity set size is only 2, so privacy depth is modest

Final read:
Valid CoinJoin pattern with minimal but real ambiguity.
data.title: Text Info Node
False Positive Risk
data.comment: Case B is LOW_FP_RISK because structure includes multiple inputs plus realistic change behavior.

data.content:
### Case B Verdict: Strong Practical Positive

What passes:
- multi-party ownership structure
- equal-output and ratio checks
- change-band plausibility
- stricter confidence gates

Final read:
Best example of realistic CoinJoin behavior in this lesson.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
### Case C Verdict: High-Confidence False Positive

Why it fails despite strong output metrics:
- one-input ownership breaks the multi-party premise
- false-positive risk rises sharply
- apparent anonymity does not represent independent participants

Final read:
Output-strong, structure-failed, not CoinJoin.
data.title: Text Info Node
B Remix Depth Score
data.comment: Summarizes fresh vs remixed input depth for long-run privacy strength.

data.title: C Collector Pattern Score
data.comment: Measures post-mix anonymity loss from downstream output co-spending.

data.content:
### Post-Mix Caveat: Collector Pattern

Even after a valid mix, privacy can degrade later.
If multiple outputs are co-spent by one downstream owner cluster, linkability can return.

This lane models that retroactive collapse so apparent anonymity is not mistaken for durable anonymity.
data.title: Text Info Node
B Script Fingerprint
data.comment: Evaluates native-segwit consistency as an implementation fingerprint signal.

data.title: B Star Pattern Score
data.comment: Computes effective anonymity under shared input-source concentration.

data.content:
### Provenance Caveat: Star Pattern

A transaction can look valid while upstream funding is concentrated.
If several inputs trace to one controller, effective anonymity shrinks.

So this lane combines:
- apparent output anonymity
- input-source diversity

Result: a context-aware anonymity estimate, not just a cosmetic one.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
## Summary: CoinJoin Classification Recipe

| Signal | Case A | Case B | Case C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input ownership | 2 owners | 5 owners | 1 owner |
| Equal outputs | yes | yes | yes |
| Change behavior | none | realistic | payout-like |
| False-positive risk | low-medium | low | high |
| Effective anonymity | limited | strongest | near-zero |

Analyst workflow:
1. Establish independent ownership.
2. Validate denomination and change structure.
3. Run heuristic gates (anon set, ratio, false-positive score).
4. Adjust to effective anonymity using provenance and downstream context.

Bottom line:
Heuristics can support a CoinJoin verdict.
They cannot replace structure.
data.title: Text Info Node

data.content:
## Research Context: Adoption Scale

Observed datasets show meaningful scale:
- 30,251 detected Wasabi transactions
- 223,597 detected Samourai transactions
- 227,480.84 BTC mixed in aggregate (about 4.74B USD at study valuation)

Recent monthly averages remained substantial:
- about 172.93M USD (Wasabi)
- about 41.72M USD (Samourai)

Why this matters:
Classification quality is an operational requirement, not a niche exercise.

## Research Context: Detection Performance

Published evaluations report very high detection quality in architecture-aware settings:
- Wasabi pipelines around 99%+ in reported experiments
- Samourai tracing can approach near-deterministic behavior when model assumptions hold

Method takeaway:
Output heuristics alone are insufficient.
Architecture and context features materially improve reliability.

## Research Context: Exchange Exposure

CoinJoin-linked value has been observed reaching exchanges through:
- direct deposits
- short-hop chains

Reported lower-bound findings include:
- 13,899 BTC direct acceptance
- 54,468 BTC indirect acceptance

Operational implication:
CoinJoin classification intersects with compliance, risk scoring, and provenance workflows.

## Research Context: Policy Tension

Regulatory guidance often treats mixing activity as a risk signal.
At the same time, many users seek CoinJoin for legitimate privacy goals.

Operational takeaway:
- privacy and compliance incentives can conflict
- over-detection and under-detection both carry cost
- structure-first, evidence-based analysis is the safest path
data.title: Text Info Node
